county_metadata.research_tips: Platte County Clerk in Wheatland has records from 1911. The Wheatland Colony irrigation project (1883) was one of Wyoming's earliest — colony company records identify original settlers. Platte County Library has local genealogy materials. Fort Laramie records cover the eastern portion of what became Platte County. The Guernsey Ruts (Oregon Trail) are in Platte County — emigrant registers document overland travelers. Check Laramie County for pre-1911 records. The Chicago, Burlington & Quincy Railroad opened the area to settlement; railroad land sale records identify homesteaders. Hartville mining district records document early miners.

county_metadata.research_tips: Sheridan County Clerk in Sheridan holds records from 1888. Sheridan County Museum has strong local history collections. The Sheridan Inn (est. 1893) was a social center — hotel registers may document visitors and residents. Coal mining at Dietz and Monarch drew diverse immigrant workers including Polish, Italian, and Slavic families — mine employment records at WY State Archives. Fort Mackenzie VA facility records (1903+) at NARA. King Saddlery and ranch supply records document ranching families. Bradford Brinton Memorial ranch near Big Horn has papers documenting the ranching elite. Trail End State Historic Site has Kendrick family papers. The Tongue River valley attracted early stockmen from Texas trail drives.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1921 from Lincoln and Fremont counties. One of Wyoming's youngest counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sublette County Clerk in Pinedale has records from 1921. Museum of the Mountain Man in Pinedale documents the fur trade era (1820s-1840s) — rendezvous sites along the Green River are within the county. Sublette County was the last Wyoming county organized; check Lincoln and Fremont counties for pre-1921 records. Ranching families dominated early settlement; the Upper Green River Cattlemen's Association records identify stockmen. The Sublette County Library has local family files. Tie hack (railroad tie cutters) camps employed Scandinavian workers whose records may be in forest service files at NARA. Very sparse population makes newspaper research particularly productive.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1867 as one of Wyoming's original counties before territorial organization. Fremont, Sublette, and other counties carved from it.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sweetwater County Clerk in Green River has records from 1867. Rock Springs and Green River were Union Pacific railroad towns — UP employment records are critical. The Rock Springs Chinese Massacre (1885) destroyed the Chinatown; survivors' claims at NARA document the Chinese community. Rock Springs Historical Museum has coal mining and ethnic community records. Sweetwater County attracted Finnish, Italian, Greek, Japanese, and Chinese miners — ethnic fraternal organization records may survive. The Overland Trail crossed the county. South Pass City historic site preserves gold rush-era records. Sweetwater County Museum has pioneer family files. Trona mining records (post-1940s) document later families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1921 from Lincoln County. Contains Grand Teton National Park and the southern entrance to Yellowstone.
county_metadata.research_tips: Teton County Clerk in Jackson has records from 1921. Jackson Hole Historical Society and Museum has extensive family papers, photographs, and oral histories of homesteading families. Teton County's isolation meant homesteaders arrived late (1880s-1900s); many came from Idaho and Utah. Grand Teton National Park records at NARA document dude ranches, outfitting operations, and early tourism. Check Lincoln County for pre-1921 records. The Jackson Hole Courier newspaper (1909+) is an excellent genealogical source for the valley. Elk refuge and Forest Service records at NARA may document early residents. Several families fought the federal government over park expansion — legal records at the National Archives document these disputes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869, one of the original Wyoming Territory counties. Lincoln and Sublette later carved from Uinta.
county_metadata.research_tips: Uinta County Clerk in Evanston has some of the oldest records in Wyoming, dating to 1869. Evanston was a major Union Pacific railroad division point; roundhouse and shop employment records at UP archives in Omaha. Chinese railroad workers established a Joss House (temple) in Evanston — records of the Chinese community at the WY State Archives. Uinta County Museum has local genealogy collections. Fort Bridger State Historic Site preserves military post and trading post records; fort records at NARA cover 1858-1890. Mormon settlers from Utah crossed into Uinta County; LDS ward records on FamilySearch are essential. The Evanston State Hospital (1887) patient records are restricted but may be accessible for genealogical purposes through the WY State Archives.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from Big Horn County. Named for Shoshone chief Washakie.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washakie County Clerk in Worland holds records from 1911. The Washakie Museum and Cultural Center in Worland has pioneer family files and photographs. The Big Horn Basin colonization (1890s-1910s) brought homesteaders to irrigated lands along the Big Horn River; Bureau of Reclamation project records at NARA Denver identify water right holders. German-Russian and Mormon farming communities are well-documented. Check Big Horn County for pre-1911 records. The Chicago, Burlington & Quincy Railroad brought settlers; railroad land office records identify purchasers. Sugar beet farming attracted seasonal laborers — Holly Sugar employment records may survive. Worland town records date to incorporation in 1906.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1890 from Crook County. Campbell County later carved from Weston.
county_metadata.research_tips: Weston County Clerk in Newcastle has records from 1890. Newcastle was a coal mining town on the Burlington Railroad — mine company records and railroad employment files are at the WY State Archives and Burlington Northern Santa Fe archives. The Black Hills gold rush overflow brought prospectors and settlers in the 1870s-80s — check Crook County and Lawrence County, South Dakota for related records. Anna Miller Museum in Newcastle has excellent local genealogy collections. Cambria coal mines employed diverse immigrant workers. Check Crook County for pre-1890 records. The Cheyenne-Deadwood stage route crossed Weston County; stage station records survive. Oil discoveries at the Lance Creek field (1918) brought oil workers documented in State Oil and Gas Commission files.
